tossups.question: This movement was arguably launched by a poem titled for a remark about “an only daughter” in The Madness of Suibhne [“SIV-nuh”]. A train journey from Rome to Paris prompts a discussion of dwindling Catholic faith in the first poem of a collection from this movement, whose name comes from a review by M. L. Rosenthal. “Beyond the Alps,” “91 Revere Street,” and (*) “Heart’s Needle” were early pieces in this movement of a poem that declares, “White Godiva, I unpeel” after opening, “Stasis in darkness.” That poem, which ends with the image of “the red eye, the cauldron of morning,” titles a collection edited by Ted Hughes and named for the poet’s horse, Ariel. For 10 points, Robert Lowell.and Sylvia Plath belong to what movement of frequently autobiographical poetry?
tossups.question_sanitized: This movement was arguably launched by a poem titled for a remark about "an only daughter" in The Madness of Suibhne ["SIV-nuh"]. A train journey from Rome to Paris prompts a discussion of dwindling Catholic faith in the first poem of a collection from this movement, whose name comes from a review by M. L. Rosenthal. "Beyond the Alps," "91 Revere Street," and (*) "Heart's Needle" were early pieces in this movement of a poem that declares, "White Godiva, I unpeel" after opening, "Stasis in darkness." That poem, which ends with the image of "the red eye, the cauldron of morning," titles a collection edited by Ted Hughes and named for the poet's horse, Ariel. For 10 points, Robert Lowell.and Sylvia Plath belong to what movement of frequently autobiographical poetry?
tossups.answer: Confessional poetry [or Confessionalism; or Confessionalists] (the first two poems mentioned are W. D. Snodgrass’s “Heart’s Needle” and Robert Lowell’s “Beyond the Alps”)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Confessional poetry [or Confessionalism; or Confessionalists] (the first two poems mentioned are W. D. Snodgrass's "Heart's Needle" and Robert Lowell's "Beyond the Alps")

tossups.question: A poem from this literary movement states that “I would like a simple life” and describes “all night dark wings flapping in my heart”. Besides “The Ambition Bird”, another poem from this movement tells of the “pheasant cocks that march past their dubious hens'' and ends with the claim that “You are still my daughter”. That poem was dedicated to “ Cynthia'' and is interspersed with imagery from the Korean War. One poem from this movement describes “where the graveyard shelves on the town” and a “red fox stain” that covers “Blue Hill”. A radio bleats (*) “Love, O careless Love. . .” and the narrator states that, “I myself am Hell” in a poem from this movement. For 10 points, name this movement characterized by its use of direct and colloquial style whose members included W.D Snodgrass, Robert Lowell, and Anne Sexton.
tossups.question_sanitized: A poem from this literary movement states that "I would like a simple life" and describes "all night dark wings flapping in my heart". Besides "The Ambition Bird", another poem from this movement tells of the "pheasant cocks that march past their dubious hens'' and ends with the claim that "You are still my daughter". That poem was dedicated to " Cynthia'' and is interspersed with imagery from the Korean War. One poem from this movement describes "where the graveyard shelves on the town" and a "red fox stain" that covers "Blue Hill". A radio bleats (*) "Love, O careless Love. . ." and the narrator states that, "I myself am Hell" in a poem from this movement. For 10 points, name this movement characterized by its use of direct and colloquial style whose members included W.D Snodgrass, Robert Lowell, and Anne Sexton.

tossups.question: The term for this poetic movement was coined in a review by M.L. Rosenthal. A poem from this movement states, “A woman like that is not a woman” and declares, “I have been her kind,” while another poem from the same collection from this movement describes its author’s mental state “Part way back from Bedlam.” A poem from a movement named for this style describes construction near the Boston Aquarium disturbing a monument to (*) Colonel Shaw. A poet from this movement published the collections The Awful Rowing Toward God, while a different, male member of this movement wrote the poem “For the Union Dead.” For 10 points, Anne Sexton, Robert Lowell, and Sylvia Plath belonged to what poetic movement of the 1950s which focused on personal experience and trauma?
tossups.question_sanitized: The term for this poetic movement was coined in a review by M.L. Rosenthal. A poem from this movement states, "A woman like that is not a woman" and declares, "I have been her kind," while another poem from the same collection from this movement describes its author's mental state "Part way back from Bedlam." A poem from a movement named for this style describes construction near the Boston Aquarium disturbing a monument to (*) Colonel Shaw. A poet from this movement published the collections The Awful Rowing Toward God, while a different, male member of this movement wrote the poem "For the Union Dead." For 10 points, Anne Sexton, Robert Lowell, and Sylvia Plath belonged to what poetic movement of the 1950s which focused on personal experience and trauma?

bonuses.leadin: The speaker states “There ought to be a law against [this character]” in a poem that begins “Filling her compact &/ delicious body / with chicken páprika, she glanced at me / twice.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this “huffy” character with “plights and gripes as bad as Achilles.” He appears in a cycle of poems that includes His Toy, His Dream, His Rest.
John Berryman used Henry as an authorial stand-in in this autobiographical group of poems, which includes musings on depression and controversial “blackface” passages.

Berryman’s Dream Songs are often grouped with this postwar style of American poetry. Associated with Sylvia Plath and Robert Lowell, it focuses on the personal disclosure of extreme experience or trauma.

bonuses.leadin: The works of Rainer Maria Rilke have spawned a variety of poetic responses. For 10 points each:
parts: John Berryman flatly asserts “Rilke was a jerk” in an entry of his Dream Songs, a landmark collection in this poetic style that emerged during the 1950s. Its name was coined in a review of Robert Lowell’s Life Studies.

Rilke’s “thing-poems” prompted this writer to call him the “Santa Claus of loneliness.” This writer’s review “Rilke in English” was published four days after the title date of his poem about World War II, “September 1, 1939.”

Denise Levertov’s many Rilke-inspired works include two poems whose title begins with this word. In a parody of William Carlos Williams whose title begins with this word, the speaker chops down a house and sprays some hollyhocks with lye.
answers: variations [or variations on a theme; or theme and variations; accept “Variations on a Theme by Rilke” or “Variations on a Theme by William Carlos Williams”] (The second poem is by Kenneth Koch.)
answers_sanitized: W. H. Auden [or Wystan Hugh Auden]
variations [or variations on a theme; or theme and variations; accept "Variations on a Theme by Rilke" or "Variations on a Theme by William Carlos Williams"] (The second poem is by Kenneth Koch.)

bonuses.leadin: A poem titled for this action states that "I was stamped out like a Plymouth fender into this world" and concludes that a "story ends with me still" performing this action. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this action. The last poem in a collection titled for this "awful" action describes the speaker getting a royal flush in poker after arriving at "the island called God."
This poet wrote An Awful Rowing Toward God. This author of "Wanting to Die" proclaimed, "I have gone out, a possessed witch" to open the poem "Her Kind," collected in To Bedlam and Part Way Back.
Like Sharon Olds and Robert Lowell, Sexton was a member of this movement of the 1950s and 1960s, whose members wrote poetry focused on individual experience and personal trauma.
answers: rowing [or An Awful Rowing Toward God; or "The Rowing Endeth"]
answers_sanitized: rowing [or An Awful Rowing Toward God; or "The Rowing Endeth"]

bonuses.leadin: A poem from this movement describes how suicides, like carpenters, “want to know which tools. They never ask why build.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poetic movement that critics coined in reviews of the collection Life Studies, which features a poem titled for an animal who “jabs her wedge-head in a cup of sour cream.”
This surname was shared by Robert, the Confessional poet who included “Skunk Hour” in Life Studies, and Amy, who wrote the Imagist poems “Lilacs” and “Patterns.”

A Robert Lowell poem titled for this other poet describes him asking, “why did we live? Why do we die?” The speaker of a poem by this author describes being “hunched” in the belly of the state “till my wet fur froze.”
answers: Confessional poetry [or Confessionalism; accept “Poetry as Confession”] (The poem in the first line is Anne Sexton’s “Wanting to Die.”)
Lowell [accept Robert Lowell or Robert Traill Spence Lowell IV; accept Amy Lowell or Amy Lawrence Lowell]
Randall Jarrell (The poem is “The Death of the Ball Turret Gunner.”)

packet.name: 11 - Cambridge A, Sheffield A, Purdue B, Princeton A

Randall Jarrell (The poem is "The Death of the Ball Turret Gunner.")

bonuses.leadin: This literary movement produced works which emphasized topics like personal emotional trauma, mental illness, and sexuality. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this American poetry movement exemplified by writer such as Robert Lowell and Sylvia Plath.
John Berryman, a confessional poet, is best known for this collection focused on Henry, a character who sometimes appears in blackface and is occasionally addressed as Mr. Bones.
Berryman was heavily introduced by this poet and short story author. His most famous story is “In Dreams Begin Responsibilities” which was inspired by the broken marriage of his parents.

bonuses.leadin: Delmore Schwartz’s unsuccessful epic Genesis was an early example of a poem written in this style, whose name was coined by the critic M. L. Rosenthal. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this style of poetry used by the frighteningly candid Sharon Olds, and by Robert Lowell in many poems from his collection Life Studies.

In 1960, Life Studies won the National Book Award and the Pulitzer Prize went to this collection by W.D. Snodgrass, which alternates images of the Korean War with those illustrating the author’s divorce and separation from his daughter.
This author bared her soul in poems like “Lady Lazarus,” “Ariel,” and “Daddy” prior to committing suicide in 1963.

This author bared her soul in poems like "Lady Lazarus," "Ariel," and "Daddy" prior to committing suicide in 1963.

bonuses.leadin: This collection takes its epigraph and title from The Madness of Suibhne. FTPE:
parts: Name this ten-poem sequence, inspired by the author’s separation from his daughter, Cynthia Jean, whom he refers to as “Child of my winter”. It won its author the 1960 Pulitzer Prize for Poetry.
Heart’s Needle is by this American poet, who wrote a series of poetic monologues from the perspective of Hitler during the last days of WWII in The Führer Bunker.

Heart’s Needle was one of the most influential works in this movement of poetry, which focuses on intimate and often dark details of the poet’s life. Other collections in this movement include Sylvia Plath’s Ariel and Anne Sexton's work.
